questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation in memory management.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated memory blocks due to fixed partition/page sizes
External fragmentation: free memory scattered in non-contiguous blocks throughout memory
Internal occurs in paging and fixed partitioning; external occurs in variable partitioning
Compaction addresses external fragmentation; paging eliminates it

questions.question: Describe the steps that occur when a page fault is generated.
keyPoints: Page fault occurs when process accesses a page not in physical memory
OS must find a free frame or evict an existing page
If frame is dirty, write it to disk (page replacement)
Load required page from secondary storage into the free frame
Update page table entry with new frame number and valid bit
Restart the interrupted instruction

questions.question: What are the advantages of paging over contiguous memory allocation?
keyPoints: Eliminates external fragmentation completely
Allows non-contiguous allocation of physical memory
Simplifies memory allocation (only frame allocation needed)
Facilitates sharing of code and data between processes
Enables virtual memory allowing programs larger than physical memory

questions.question: Explain the working set model for memory management.
keyPoints: Working set is the set of pages a process is actively using
Defined by window size - pages used in last n references
Processthrashes if allocated frames < working set size
Goal: keep working set in memory to prevent thrashing
OS monitors and adjusts frame allocation based on working set

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? How does it improve performance?
keyPoints: TLB is a hardware cache for page table entries
Stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations
Avoids expensive page table lookups in main memory
On TLB hit: O(1) address translation
On TLB miss: must access page table then update TLB
Typical hit rate: 90-99% making average access time close to cache speed

questions.question: Differentiate between paging and segmentation.
keyPoints: Paging: fixed-size blocks (pages/frames), eliminates external fragmentation
Segmentation: variable-sized logical divisions (code, data, stack)
Segmentation provides logical view of memory with meaningful segments
Paging is transparent to programmer; segmentation requires programmer awareness
Both can be combined: segmented paging or paged segments

questions.question: What is virtual memory? How does demand paging implement virtual memory?
keyPoints: Virtual memory gives illusion of more memory than physically available
Programs can be larger than actual RAM
Demand paging loads pages only when referenced (on demand)
Pages not in memory marked 'not present' in page table
Page fault handler loads pages from disk as needed
Allows execution of programs larger than physical memory

questions.question: Explain the various page replacement algorithms. Compare FIFO, Optimal, and LRU algorithms with respect to page faults and practical implementation.
keyPoints: FIFO: Evicts oldest page in memory - simple but suffers from Belady's anomaly
Optimal (OPT): Evicts page that will not be used longest - impossible to implement but theoretical benchmark
LRU: Evicts least recently used page - good approximation of OPT but requires hardware support
Belady's anomaly: FIFO can have more page faults with more frames
LRU implementation: counters or stack algorithm approximations
Clock (Second Chance) algorithm: practical LRU approximation using reference bit
Performance: OPT < LRU < Clock < FIFO in most cases

questions.question: Describe the structure and types of page tables. Explain multi-level page tables and their advantages.
keyPoints: Single-level page table: one entry per virtual page - becomes huge for large address spaces
Multi-level page tables: hierarchical structure - outer page table points to inner page tables
Inverted page tables: one entry per physical frame instead of virtual page
Hashed page tables: use hash function for virtual page to physical frame mapping
Advantages of multi-level: only allocate page tables for valid pages, reduces memory overhead
Each level adds one memory access for translation (more translation time)
TLB helps mitigate multi-level page table overhead

questions.question: Explain the concept of thrashing. How does the operating system detect and prevent thrashing?
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive page fault activity causing CPU to spend more time on paging than execution
Symptoms: CPU utilization drops, disk I/O increases sharply, processes compete for too few frames
Detection: monitor page fault frequency and CPU utilization
Prevention strategies: working set model, page fault frequency approach
Working set: keep active pages in memory based on recent reference history
Page fault frequency: maintain fault rate within acceptable range by adjusting allocated frames
Local vs global replacement: local prevents one process affecting others
Some systems use process suspension to recover from thrashing
